4.1: We have been entrusted with this ministry—and that only because of God's mercy toward us. So we do not lose heart.
4.2: We have put away every secret disgrace and every underhanded scheme. We never tamper with God's word to make it say what people want to hear. Instead, by declaring the truth openly and honestly, we let every person's conscience be the judge—under God's own gaze.
4.3: If our message is hidden at all, it is hidden only to those who are on the road to destruction.
4.4: The ruler of this present age has blinded the minds of those who refuse to believe, keeping them from seeing the blazing light of the good news—the glory of Christ, who is himself the image of God.
4.5: We do not proclaim ourselves. We proclaim Jesus Christ as Lord—and ourselves as nothing more than your servants for Jesus' sake.
4.6: The same God who commanded light to blaze out of the primordial darkness has flooded our hearts with light—the light that reveals the knowledge of God's glory as it shines in the face of Jesus Christ.
4.7: Yet we carry this priceless treasure in ordinary clay jars—so that the extraordinary power at work in us is unmistakably God's and not our own.
4.8: We are pressed from every direction—but not crushed. We are bewildered—but never in complete despair.
4.9: We are hunted—but never abandoned. We are knocked to the ground—but never finished off.
4.10: At every moment we carry in our bodies the dying of Jesus—so that the life of Jesus may also be seen at work in our mortal bodies.
4.11: We who are still alive are being handed over to death again and again—for Jesus' sake—so that his life might break through into this dying flesh.
4.12: Death is doing its work in us—but life is flowing to you.
4.13: Scripture says, 'I believed, and so I spoke.' We have that same spirit of faith—we believe, and so we speak.
4.14: We know this: the God who raised the Lord Jesus from the dead will also raise us up with Jesus—and will bring all of us, you included, into his presence.
4.15: Everything that happens to us is for your sake—so that as grace spreads to more and more people, the flood of thanksgiving may rise and overflow, all to the glory of God.
4.16: So we do not give up. Even though our bodies are wearing out, the inner person is being made new every single day.
4.17: Our present troubles are slight and short-lived compared to the immeasurable, unending weight of glory being produced in us through them.
4.18: We keep our gaze on what cannot be seen rather than on what can—because the visible world is passing away, while what is invisible lasts forever.

5.1: We know this: if the tent we now live in—our earthly body—is torn down, we have a permanent home waiting from God, a dwelling place in the heavens, built without human hands, built to last forever.
5.2: In this tent we groan—aching to put on our heavenly home like a garment over us,
5.3: for when that happens we will be fully clothed, not stripped bare.
5.4: We are burdened inside this tent—we groan under the weight—not because we want to shed our bodies, but because we want to be further clothed, so that what is mortal and dying in us might be absorbed into life itself.
5.5: And God is the one who has been shaping us all along for this very destiny. As proof of his intention, he has given us his Spirit as a first installment—the down payment on everything that is coming.
5.6: So we are always full of courage. We know that while we make our home in this body, we are away from our true home—away from the Lord.
5.7: This is the only way we travel: by trusting what we cannot see, not by what our eyes can observe.
5.8: Yes, we are full of courage—and we would far rather leave this body and be home with the Lord.
5.9: Whether we are in the body or out of it, we share one ambition: to please him.
5.10: For we will all stand before Christ's judgment seat—every one of us. Each person will give an account for what they did with their life in this body, good or bad.
5.11: Knowing what it is to stand in awe before the Lord, we work to persuade others. God already sees us for what we are—and I hope your own conscience also recognizes the truth about us.
5.12: We are not promoting ourselves again. We are giving you reason to be proud of us—so that you will have something to say when people boast about surface appearances rather than what is in the heart.
5.13: If we seem out of our minds, it is before God that we are so—and it is for his sake. If we are sober and collected, it is for yours.
5.14: Christ's love has seized hold of us—for we have reached this conclusion: one man died for all, which means that in him all died.
5.15: He died for all of them—so that those who are now alive would stop living for themselves and start living for the one who died and rose again for them.
5.16: From this point on, then, we no longer evaluate anyone from the outside-in, by merely human standards. We once thought about Christ that way—but we have left that way of seeing behind.
5.17: Anyone who is in Christ has entered the new creation. The old order of things is gone. Look—everything has become new!
5.18: All this comes from God. He is the one who, through Christ, brought us back into relationship with himself—and then handed us the task of bringing others into that same reconciliation.
5.19: Here is what that reconciliation means: in Christ, God was making peace with the whole world—not holding anyone's sins against them. And he has entrusted us with the proclamation of that peace.
5.20: We are official envoys on Christ's behalf—and when we speak, God himself is making the appeal through us. We urge you, as Christ's representatives: accept the reconciliation God is offering.
5.21: The sinless one he made to carry our sin—so that in him we might stand before God clothed in his own righteousness.

6.1: As those who work alongside God, we beg you: do not let the grace he has given you go to waste.
6.2: For God says in Scripture: 'At the right moment I answered you; on the day of salvation I came to your aid.' That right moment is now. Today is the day of salvation.
6.3: We do nothing that would put an obstacle in anyone's way—we will not allow the ministry to be brought into disrepute.
6.4: Instead, we show ourselves true servants of God in every circumstance—by patient endurance through troubles, hardships, and desperate situations;
6.5: through beatings, imprisonments, and riots; through exhausting labor, sleepless nights, and going without food;
6.6: by purity of life, clear understanding, patient endurance, and genuine kindness; by the power of the Holy Spirit and by authentic love;
6.7: with the word of truth and the power of God as our weapons; with righteousness as our armor in both hands—for both attack and defense;
6.8: through honor and disgrace, through slander and praise—written off as frauds, yet completely honest;
6.9: dismissed as nobodies yet known to everyone; on the edge of death, yet still alive; beaten within an inch of our lives, yet never actually killed;
6.10: broken-hearted, yet always full of joy; penniless, yet enriching many; owning nothing, yet possessing everything.
6.11: Corinthians, we have spoken plainly with you—our hearts have been thrown wide open to you.
6.12: We have not closed our hearts to you—you are the ones who have closed yours to us.
6.13: As a fair return—and I am speaking to you as my own children—open your hearts wide to us as well.
6.14: Do not link your life to unbelievers as though you were equal partners. What bond can there be between righteousness and wickedness? What common ground between light and darkness?
6.15: What harmony can there be between Christ and Belial, the enemy of all good? What shared life exists between someone who trusts God and someone who does not?
6.16: What agreement can exist between the temple of God and the shrine of an idol? We ourselves are the temple of the living God—and God has said: 'I will make my home among them. I will walk with them. I will be their God, and they will be my people.'
6.17: 'Come out from among them,' says the Lord. 'Keep yourself separate. Do not touch what is defiled—and I will take you to myself.'
6.18: 'I will be your Father,' says the Lord Almighty—'and you will be my sons and daughters.'

1.1: From Paul, apostle of Christ Jesus by God's own appointment, and from our brother Timothy. To the congregation of God at Corinth — and to all God's people throughout Achaia.
1.2: May God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ give you grace and peace.
1.3: All praise to the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ. He is the Father of compassion and the source of every comfort —
1.4: who comforts us in every suffering we face, so that we in turn can bring to others the same comfort God has poured into us.
1.5: We share abundantly in Christ's sufferings — and in exactly that measure, God's comfort flows to us through Christ.
1.6: When we endure suffering, it is for your comfort and salvation. When we receive comfort, it is for your comfort too — comfort that equips you to endure patiently the same trials we face.
1.7: Our hope for you stands firm: because you share our sufferings, you will equally share our comfort.
1.8: We want you to know about the crushing ordeal we went through in Asia. We were overwhelmed beyond all bearing — the pressure so severe that we gave up hope of surviving.
1.9: We felt the death sentence in our own hearts — and that was precisely the point. God was stripping us of all self-reliance, teaching us to trust in him alone: the God who raises the dead.
1.10: He rescued us from that mortal danger, and he delivers us still. We have placed our hope in him, and he will go on rescuing us.
1.11: You play your part by praying for us. Then when God grants us the gift, many people will offer him thanks — all because so many prayed.
1.12: Here is what we are proud of: our conscience bears witness that we have lived in the world — and especially in our dealings with you — with transparent honesty that comes from God. We have not relied on human cleverness; we have relied on God's grace.
1.13: Everything we have written to you is straightforward — nothing hidden, nothing between the lines. And I hope that,
1.14: just as you have partly come to understand us, you will one day understand us fully — that on the day of the Lord Jesus we can take pride in you, and you in us.
1.15: It was this confidence that made me plan to visit you first — I wanted you to receive a double gift of grace.
1.16: My plan was to stop with you on the way to Macedonia, come back through you on the return, and then have you send me on to Judea — two visits instead of one.
1.17: Does this change of plan make me a waverer — someone who can't be trusted to mean what he says? Do I operate by purely human calculation, saying yes and no in the same breath?
1.18: I call God himself to witness: what we have spoken to you has never been a mixture of yes and no.
1.19: The Son of God, Jesus Christ — whom Silvanus, Timothy, and I proclaimed to you — was never a mixture of yes and no. In him, there is only Yes.
1.20: Every promise God ever made finds its fulfillment in Christ — every last one is Yes. And so it is through Christ that we say Amen to God, to his glory.
1.21: It is God himself who has made us firm together with you in Christ. He anointed us —
1.22: stamped his ownership on us and planted his Spirit in our hearts as the down payment on all he has promised.
1.23: God is my witness — I put my very soul on the line: the reason I have not yet come back to Corinth is to spare you grief.
1.24: This is not about us ruling your faith — that is not our authority. We are partners in your joy. After all, you already stand firm in your faith.

2.1: I made a firm decision: I was not going to pay you another grief-laden visit.
2.2: Think about it: if I make you sad, who will cheer me up? Only the very people I have made sad.
2.3: That is why I wrote instead of visiting: I did not want to arrive and be grieved by the very people who ought to fill me with joy. I was — and still am — convinced that when I rejoice, you all rejoice with me.
2.4: When I wrote that letter, I was in great anguish of heart — tears came with every word. My aim was not to hurt you; I wrote so that you would understand the depth of love I have for you.
2.5: The person who caused the hurt did not really hurt me alone — in some degree he hurt all of you. I say 'in some degree' so as not to lay it on too thick.
2.6: The censure the community imposed on that person is punishment enough.
2.7: It is time to reverse course: forgive him, welcome him back, encourage him — or excessive grief may swallow him whole.
2.8: I am urging you: make it unmistakably clear to him that you love him.
2.9: This was also part of my purpose in writing: I wanted to see whether you would rise to the test — whether you are truly obedient in everything.
2.10: Whatever you forgive, I forgive too. And where I have forgiven anything, I did it for your sake, in the sight of Christ himself.
2.11: We must not give Satan an opening — we know how he operates.
2.12: When I arrived in Troas to preach the gospel of Christ, the Lord had set wide open a door of opportunity —
2.13: but I had no peace of mind, because Titus my brother was not there. So I took my leave and pressed on to Macedonia.
2.14: Praise to God! He continually leads us forward as trophies in Christ's victory procession, and through us he spreads everywhere the fragrance of the knowledge of Christ — like incense filling every room.
2.15: We are to God the very fragrance of Christ — whether those who encounter us are on the path to life or the path to death.
2.16: For those who are perishing, we carry the smell of death leading to death; for those being saved, the smell of life leading to life. And who on earth is adequate for such a mission?
2.17: We are not like the many who hawk God's word for personal gain. We are people of integrity, commissioned by God, speaking in Christ's name with God himself as our witness.

3.1: Are we starting all over to put ourselves forward? Do we need — as some apparently do — commendation letters to bring to you, or letters from you to carry elsewhere?
3.2: You are our letter — inscribed on our hearts, visible to everyone, open for all the world to read.
3.3: You are a letter written by Christ himself — delivered through our hands, penned not with ink but by the Spirit of the living God, inscribed not on stone tablets but on the living tablets of the human heart.
3.4: This is the confidence we have — and it flows entirely through Christ, toward God.
3.5: It is not that we are in any way adequate on our own — we cannot even have a useful thought that originates in ourselves. Our adequacy is entirely God's gift.
3.6: He has fitted us to serve the new covenant — not the written code, but the Spirit. For the written code executes; the Spirit makes alive.
3.7: Consider: the ministry inscribed on stone — the very ministry that brought death — was launched in such blazing glory that the Israelites could not look Moses in the face. And that glory was already fading.
3.8: How much more glorious, then, must be the ministry of the Spirit!
3.9: If the ministry that condemned people was launched in glory, the ministry that declares people righteous surpasses it in glory beyond measure.
3.10: In fact, what was once glorious has lost all appearance of glory — completely eclipsed by a glory so overwhelming that it reduces the former to nothing.
3.11: If what was temporary came wrapped in glory, the permanent surpasses it in glory beyond all comparison.
3.12: Because we hold this hope, we speak with great freedom and openness.
3.13: We are nothing like Moses, who hid his face behind a veil so that Israel could not watch the fading glory reach its end.
3.14: But their minds were hardened — calcified. To this very day, when they read the old covenant, that veil lies there unlifted. Only in Christ does it fall away.
3.15: Even now, every time Moses is read aloud, a veil lies over their hearts.
3.16: But the moment someone turns to the Lord, the veil lifts.
3.17: The Lord — the one you turn to — is the Spirit. And wherever the Spirit of the Lord is present, there is freedom.
3.18: And all of us — with unveiled faces — are gazing on the Lord's glory as in a mirror, and we are being transformed from one degree of glory to the next into that same image. This is the work of the Lord, who is the Spirit.

13.1: I am coming to you for the third time. And Scripture's standard applies here: no accusation stands unless it is confirmed by two or three witnesses.
13.2: When I was there the second time I gave a clear warning. I am giving it again now, from a distance—to those who have been living in sin and to everyone else: if I come back, I will not hold back.
13.3: You want proof that Christ really speaks through me? Then consider this: Christ does not act weakly toward you—he exercises his full power in your midst.
13.4: He was crucified as an act of weakness—but he now lives through the sheer power of God. We share in that weakness, yes; but when we must deal with you, we will be fully alive with him in the power of God.
13.5: Stop putting me to the test and examine yourselves instead. Are you actually living in the faith? Put yourselves to the test. Surely you know that Jesus Christ lives in you—unless you have already disqualified yourselves.
13.6: And I am confident that when you examine us honestly, you will see we have not been disqualified.
13.7: Our prayer to God is simply that you do nothing wrong—not so that we get credit for passing some test, but because we genuinely want you to do what is right. Even if that makes us look like failures, so be it.
13.8: We have no capacity to act in opposition to the truth—only in its service.
13.9: We are entirely content to be weak if it means you are strong. In fact, that is exactly what we pray for—your full restoration and maturity in Christ.
13.10: That is why I am writing all this now, before I get there. When I arrive, I want to use the authority the Lord gave me gently—for building people up, not for knocking them down.
13.11: One last word, dear brothers and sisters: keep growing toward wholeness. Encourage each other. Be of one heart and mind. Live at peace. And the God who is love, the God who gives peace, will be with you.
13.12: Greet each other with a holy kiss—the outward sign of the life you share together.
13.13: Every member of God's family here adds their greeting to mine.
13.14: The grace that flows from the Lord Jesus Christ, the love that is the very nature of God, and the deep communion that the Holy Spirit creates—may all of this be with every one of you.

7.1: With promises like these before us, let us make a clean break from every impurity—of body and spirit alike—pressing on to complete the holiness that flows from reverence toward God.
7.2: Open your hearts to us. We wronged no one among you, exploited no one, cheated no one. We have nothing to hide.
7.3: I am not putting you on trial. You already know that you hold a place in our hearts so fixed that neither death nor life could dislodge you.
7.4: I hold nothing back with you—my confidence is unbounded, my pride in you immense. Even in the thick of all our sufferings I am overwhelmed with encouragement and brimming over with joy.
7.5: When we reached Macedonia there was no rest for us anywhere. Troubles pressed in from every direction—open conflicts in the world around us, inward anxieties within our own hearts.
7.6: But God, who never abandons the downhearted, came through for us—he sent Titus, and in Titus's arrival came the comfort we had been needing.
7.7: Not just his coming, but the news he brought: how you longed to see me, how you grieved over what had happened, how fiercely loyal to me you were. When I heard all that, my joy doubled.
7.8: That letter of mine caused you pain—I know that—and for a moment I did regret sending it. But I no longer regret it, because I can see that the grief it produced served its purpose, even if only briefly.
7.9: What gives me joy is not the pain itself but what the pain produced: it turned you around. And the grief that turned you was the right kind—grief that comes from God—so in the end you suffered no harm from it at all.
7.10: There are two kinds of grief. One is God-shaped: it moves you to genuine change and ends in life, leaving nothing to regret. The other is merely human: it cycles back on itself and leads to death.
7.11: Look at the evidence: once God-shaped grief got hold of you, the results were remarkable—urgency to set things right, a vigorous defense of your own conduct, anger at the wrong that was done, holy reverence, longing for reconciliation, passionate loyalty, a demand for accountability. You have shown yourselves, at every point, completely in the clear.
7.12: So my letter was not really about the offender or the offended. It was about you—so that you could see, standing before God, how deeply you care for us.
7.13: All of this has been a source of deep comfort to us. And above and beyond that comfort came something more: Titus's own joy—because you had revived his spirit, and his joy became ours.
7.14: Everything I told Titus about you turned out to be accurate—I was not embarrassed. Just as we have always been straight with you, so what we said to Titus has been vindicated by your conduct.
7.15: His feelings for you have grown even warmer as he recalls how fully you complied, and with what reverent, trembling care you welcomed him.
7.16: I end with joy: in every matter that counts, I am fully confident in you.

8.1: Here is something remarkable we want you to know: in the churches of Macedonia, God's grace showed up in an unexpected form.
8.2: Tested by severe hardship, pressed down by grinding poverty, they gave with an exuberant joy that produced a stunning outpouring of generosity.
8.3: No one asked them to do it. They gave to the limit of what they had, and then beyond it—I saw this myself.
8.4: in fact they came to us and pleaded—pleaded—to be allowed to have a part in the service for the saints in Jerusalem.
8.5: What they gave exceeded anything we had expected, because the first thing they gave was themselves—to the Lord, and then to us as his instruments—and that settled everything.
8.6: This is what prompted us to urge Titus to come back to you and bring to completion what he had already started—this gift, which is itself a work of grace.
8.7: You already overflow in so many ways—faith, eloquence, insight, energy, and the love we have seen growing among you. Let this act of generous giving overflow from you in the same way.
8.8: I am not issuing orders here. But by holding up the example of others, I am giving your love the chance to prove itself genuine.
8.9: The ultimate model for what I am asking you to do is this: the Lord Jesus Christ was immeasurably rich, and he stripped himself bare—for you—so that through his poverty you might inherit wealth you could never have earned.
8.10: Here is my counsel—not a command, but advice: since you were the ones who started this a year ago, and started it with eager desire, finish what you began.
8.11: The will was there from the start—now bring the deed to completion, contributing from what you actually have.
8.12: God accepts the gift in proportion to what you have, not as a shortfall against what you lack. The willingness is the thing; the amount is secondary.
8.13: I am not asking you to exhaust yourselves so that others can take it easy.
8.14: The principle is reciprocal balance: right now your surplus covers their shortage; at another time, their surplus may cover yours. The goal is not charity that creates dependence, but mutual provision that creates genuine equality.
8.15: Scripture captured this principle in the manna: in the wilderness, every morning's gathering levelled out—those who took much had no surplus, those who took little went without nothing. (Exodus 16:18)
8.16: I am grateful to God for this: he stirred the same deep concern for you in Titus's heart that burns in mine.
8.17: Titus received my appeal, but he didn't need much persuading—his own eagerness sent him to you.
8.18: With Titus we are also sending a brother whose work for the gospel has earned him a reputation across all the churches;
8.19: and not only that—the churches themselves chose him as our companion for this mission, since the whole collection is being administered for the Lord's glory and as a tangible sign of our eagerness to serve.
8.20: We have taken every precaution so that no one can accuse us of mishandling these funds—
8.21: we want what is right to be visible not only to God but to every human observer.
8.22: We are also sending another brother—someone we have tested and found trustworthy time and again—and his eagerness on this occasion is running high, because his confidence in you is strong.
8.23: To introduce them properly: Titus is my partner and fellow worker among you—fully trusted. The other brothers are envoys of the churches, bearers of Christ's honor.
8.24: Give them something to take back: let the churches see, through how you treat these men, that the love is real—and that our boasting about you was no empty praise.

9.1: On the matter of the collection for God's people in Jerusalem, further instruction from me should be unnecessary—
9.2: I have already staked my reputation on it: I told the Macedonians that Achaia was ready a year ago. And that boast lit a fire—most of them were spurred to action by your example.
9.3: Still, I am sending the brothers ahead precisely to make sure my boasting holds up. I said you were ready; I need you to be ready.
9.4: Think of how it would look: if the Macedonians arrive and find you empty-handed, we would be mortified—and so would you—after everything we said.
9.5: That is why I am sending the brothers ahead—to get everything arranged before I arrive, so that the gift you already committed to is ready and waiting, given freely from generosity and not squeezed out under pressure.
9.6: Here is the principle that governs all of this: you reap what you sow. A stingy sower goes home with a thin harvest; a generous sower fills his barn.
9.7: The only gift God delights in is the one given freely—decided in the heart beforehand, not dragged out under pressure. An eager, cheerful giver is precisely who God is looking for.
9.8: And here is what makes generosity sustainable: God himself replenishes the giver. He has the power to pour grace back into your life in such abundance that you are always fully provided for—and perpetually free to overflow into good work.
9.9: Scripture describes this person perfectly in Psalm 112: 'He gave freely, he gave to those who had nothing—and the righteousness of that giving echoes on forever.'
9.10: The God who gives the farmer his seed and gives the hungry their bread will do the same for you: he will give you more to give, and your generosity will keep bearing fruit.
9.11: As God makes you rich in every dimension of life, that richness pours out in generosity, which in turn flows back to God as the thanksgiving of those who receive it.
9.12: This act of service accomplishes two things at once: it relieves real human need, and it sends up a flood of gratitude to God. The giving does more than the giving itself.
9.13: The ripple effects will reach further than the money itself. When they receive it, those saints in Jerusalem will glorify God—not just for the gift, but because your generosity is living proof that you have truly embraced the gospel of Christ.
9.14: And you will gain something in return you could never buy: their prayers, and their hearts. They will ache with longing to know you, because they will have seen God's extraordinary grace at work in your lives.
9.15: And all of this—every strand of it—ends in the same place: thanks to God for the gift that surpasses every attempt to describe it.

10.1: It is I, Paul, who appeal to you personally, in the meekness and gentleness of Christ—I who, they say, am timid in person but bold when writing from a distance.
10.2: Please don't make me be harsh when I arrive—for I can indeed be bold against those who think that we live and operate by merely human means.
10.3: We are human beings, yes, but the battle we fight is not a human battle.
10.4: We use God's mighty weapons, not worldly ones, for knocking down the strongholds of human reasoning.
10.5: We demolish every proud obstacle that keeps people from knowing God, and we capture every rebellious thought and make it submit to Christ.
10.6: Once you are fully obedient, we will deal firmly with anyone among you who remains in rebellion.
10.7: You are looking only at what is right in front of you. If anyone is sure they belong to Christ, let them think carefully: so do we.
10.8: Even if I boast a little too freely about the authority the Lord gave us for building you up rather than tearing you down, I will not be put to shame by that boast.
10.9: I don't want it to look as though I'm trying to intimidate you with my letters.
10.10: His critics say that his letters are stern and powerful, but when he shows up in person he is a disappointment, and his preaching is not worth listening to.
10.11: Such people should understand that what we say in our letters from a distance, we will back up with action when we arrive.
10.12: We would never dare to compare ourselves to those who commend themselves so freely. When they measure their success by their own standards and compare themselves to each other—that is sheer foolishness.
10.13: We will not boast beyond the boundaries God has set for us. We will only boast about the area of work he has assigned to us, an area that reaches all the way to you.
10.14: We are not overstepping our boundaries. We were the first to bring the gospel of Christ all the way to you, so you are well within our assigned territory.
10.15: We don't take credit for the work others have done. Instead, as your faith matures, we hope that the scope of our mission will grow greatly among you.
10.16: This will allow us to carry the gospel even farther, into lands beyond you. We want to preach where no one else has laid a foundation—not to boast about what someone else has already accomplished.
10.17: As the Scripture says, 'If you want to boast, boast only about the Lord.'
10.18: The only approval that matters is the Lord's commendation—not self-congratulation.

11.1: I hope you'll be patient with me as I say some foolish things. Bear with me, please!
11.2: For I am jealous for you with the jealousy of God himself. I promised you as a pure bride to one husband—Christ.
11.3: But I am afraid that your minds may be corrupted and led away from your wholehearted devotion to Christ—just as Eve was deceived by the serpent's cunning lies.
11.4: You seem quite willing to put up with someone who comes and tells you about a different Jesus—not the one we preached—or about a different Spirit than the one you received, or a different kind of gospel.
11.5: But I don't think I am inferior in any way to these great apostles who claim such authority.
11.6: I may not be a polished speaker, but I know what I am talking about—and you know it. We have made that clear to you in every possible way.
11.7: Was it wrong of me to humble myself so that you could be lifted up? I preached God's Good News to you without charging you anything.
11.8: I was supported by other churches, essentially taking their resources in order to serve you.
11.9: When I was with you and ran short of money, I never asked you for anything. The believers from Macedonia brought me all I needed. I have never been a financial burden to you, and I never will be.
11.10: I am absolutely committed, by the truth of Christ within me, that no one in all of Greece will take away this boast of mine.
11.11: Why? Because I don't love you? God knows I do!
11.12: I will keep on doing this so as to undermine those who are looking for an opportunity to claim that their work is on the same level as ours.
11.13: These people are false apostles. They are deceitful workers who disguise themselves as apostles of Christ.
11.14: This should not surprise us. Even Satan disguises himself as an angel of light.
11.15: So it is no wonder his servants also disguise themselves as servants of righteousness. In the end they will receive the punishment their wicked deeds deserve.
11.16: I say again—please don't think of me as a fool. But even if you do, hear me out so that I too may do a little boasting.
11.17: I am not speaking as the Lord would in this matter—I am talking like a fool in my boastful confidence.
11.18: Since many others are boasting about their worldly credentials, I will too.
11.19: After all, you think you are so wise—yet you gladly put up with fools!
11.20: You put up with it when someone controls you, devours your resources, takes advantage of you, acts arrogantly toward you, or even slaps you in the face.
11.21: I'm ashamed to say that we were too 'weak' to treat you that way! But whatever those others dare to claim—I'm speaking like a fool now—I dare to claim it too.
11.22: Are they Hebrews? So am I. Are they Israelites? So am I. Are they descendants of Abraham? So am I.
11.23: Are they servants of Christ? I know I sound like a madman, but I have served him far more! I have worked harder, been put in prison more often, been whipped times without number, and faced death again and again.
11.24: Five different times the Jewish leaders gave me thirty-nine lashes.
11.25: Three times I was beaten with rods. Once I was stoned. Three times I was shipwrecked. Once I spent a whole night and a day adrift at sea.
11.26: I have traveled many weary miles and faced danger from rivers, from robbers, from my own people the Jews, and from Gentiles. I have faced danger in the cities, in the deserts, and on the seas—and from men who claim to be believers but are not.
11.27: I have worked hard and long, enduring many sleepless nights. I have been hungry and thirsty and have often gone without food. I have shivered in the cold without enough clothing to keep me warm.
11.28: Then, on top of everything else, the daily burden of my deep concern for all the churches weighs on me constantly.
11.29: Who is weak without my feeling that weakness? Who is led into sin, and I do not burn with grief and anger?
11.30: If I must boast, I would rather boast about the things that show how weak I am.
11.31: God, the Father of our Lord Jesus, who is worthy of eternal praise, knows that I am not lying.
11.32: When I was in Damascus, the governor under King Aretas had the city surrounded and wanted to capture me.
11.33: But I was let down in a basket through an opening in the city wall, and that is how I escaped from him.

12.1: This boasting is necessary even though it accomplishes nothing. So let me tell you about the visions and revelations I received from the Lord.
12.2: I was caught up to the third heaven fourteen years ago. Whether I was in my body or out of my body, I don't know—only God knows.
12.3: And I know that this man—whether in his body or away from his body, I cannot tell, but God knows—
12.4: was caught up into paradise and heard things so astounding they cannot be expressed in words—things no human being is allowed to repeat.
12.5: That experience is worth boasting about. But I am not going to boast about myself—only about my weaknesses.
12.6: If I wanted to boast I would be no fool in doing so, because I would be telling the truth. But I won't do it, because I don't want anyone to think more highly of me than what they can see in my life and work.
12.7: Even though I received such wonderful revelations from God, so I wouldn't become proud of them, I was given a thorn in my flesh—a messenger from Satan—to torment me and keep me from becoming proud.
12.8: Three times I begged the Lord to take it away from me.
12.9: Each time he said, 'My grace is all you need. My power works best in weakness.' So now I am glad to boast about my weaknesses, so that the power of Christ can work through me.
12.10: That is why I take pleasure in my weaknesses, and in the insults, hardships, persecutions, and troubles that I suffer for Christ. For when I am weak, then I am strong.
12.11: You have made me act like a fool—boasting like this. You ought to be writing commendations for me, for I am not at all inferior to these great apostles, even though I am nothing at all.
12.12: When I was with you, I certainly gave you proof that I am a true apostle. For I patiently performed many signs, wonders, and miracles among you.
12.13: The only thing I failed to do, which I do for the other churches, was to let you support me financially. Please forgive me for this terrible wrong!
12.14: Now I am coming to you for the third time, and I will not be a burden to you. I don't want what you have—I want you. Anyway, children shouldn't have to provide for their parents. Rather, parents provide for their children.
12.15: I will gladly spend everything I have, and I will even wear myself out, for the sake of your souls. If I love you more, does that mean you will love me less?
12.16: Some admit I was not a burden to you—but others still think I was sneaky and that I took advantage of you by trickery.
12.17: But tell me—did any of the men I sent to you take advantage of you?
12.18: When I urged Titus to visit you and sent our brother along with him, did Titus take advantage of you? No! And didn't we both walk in the same Spirit and follow the same course?
12.19: Perhaps you think we have been defending ourselves to you all this time. Not so! We speak in Christ's presence, before God himself—and everything we do, dear friends, is for your spiritual growth.
12.20: I am afraid that when I come I won't like what I find, and you won't like how I respond. I am afraid I will find quarreling, jealousy, outbursts of anger, selfishness, slander, gossip, arrogance, and disorderly conduct.
12.21: Yes, I am afraid that when I come again, God will humble me before you. And I will grieve over many of you who sinned before and still have not repented of your impurity, your sexual immorality, and your eagerness for lustful pleasure.
